title: Perfect 1-Factorization Conjecture (P1FC)
statement_text: The complete graph $K_n$ has a perfect 1-factorization for all even $n > 2$.
context_text: A perfect 1-factorization is a partition of the edge set of $K_n$ into perfect matchings such that the union of any two of them is a Hamiltonian cycle. The conjecture is still open in general; the paper notes that the smallest open case, according to Rosa's survey, is $n = 64$. The paper's Hamiltonicity results (Theorems 5 and 6) are conditional on P1FC.
paper_contribution: Proves that $M_{F_{Hp}}(n) = 2^{n-1}$ for all odd $n$ where P1FC holds for $K_{n+1}$ (in particular when $n = p$ or $n = 2p-1$ for an odd prime $p$), and that $M_{F_{Hc}}(n) = 2^{n-2}$ for all even $n$ where P1FC holds (in particular when $n = p+1$ or $n = 2p$ for an odd prime $p$), linking optimal structured graph-code sizes to P1FC.
notes: P1FC is explicitly named and labelled in the paper and discussed as still open in general; the paper uses it as a sufficient condition rather than making direct progress on it, but its Hamiltonicity results are fully conditional on P1FC.

title: Open case of Theorem 3 for odd $n$
statement_text: Determine $M_{F_{2c}}(n)$ for odd $n \geq 5$. The upper bound $M_{F_{2c}}(n) \leq 2^{n-2}$ holds for all $n$, but the best construction for odd $n$ yields only $2^{n-2} - \binom{n-2}{(n-3)/2}$ graphs.
context_text: Theorem 3 establishes $M_{F_{2c}}(n) = 2^{n-2}$ for all even $n$ via matching upper and lower bounds. For odd $n$ the upper bound still holds, but the construction from the even case fails; the best the authors could achieve gives $2^{n-2} - \binom{n-2}{(n-3)/2}$, leaving a gap. For $n = 3$ the upper bound is attained (a triangle and the empty graph), but no general matching construction is known.
notes: Stated as Remark 2 without a formal Problem/Conjecture environment; the authors do not explicitly conjecture the true value for odd $n$ but clearly identify it as an open case.
